title: Working through the Hyphens
abstract: I began reviewing The Unknown City: lives of poor and working class young adults by Michelle Fine and Lois Weis in the week that Britain declared its outrage at the brutal killing of a young, recently arrived, Nigerian boy-Damalola Taylor-as he walked home from school. Home for Damalola was a relatively poor, predominantly black, inner-London community that, like the two urban communities-Buffalo and NewJersey-in Fine and Weis' study, had been ravaged by the dislocations of economic and social restructuring, aggressive welfare reform, the neglect of public facilities, and the rise of drugs and crime. While at one level Damalola's death can be recorded as personal; a young life so full of promise, lost-it seemed to me the bigger tragedy was that, within days, the politicians and media could, at the same time, wring and wash their hands. In the press conferences and speeches to Parliament that followed, politicians on both sides used the event to point their finger at drugs, diminished community and lack of police numbers on the street. The political culture of 'blame-shame' reversed and re-hyphenated itself. It was the 'shame-blame' of our moral decay that needed to be policed. That such a tragedy might also be explained structurally and ideologically-as the outcome of poverty, racism and the failure of our public institutions following more than two decades of neo-liberal reform-seemed unthinkable. It was a tragedy to be moved by, but only to the increasingly practised tears of emotional self-indulgence. There was no mourning for our failure to generate the type of social critique that would galvanize us to political action. In the same week and over the Atlantic, America struggled to find a solution to its political crisis; the hyphens of 'voice-vote-value' failed to add up. The American electoral crisis threatened to expose the shakiness of the claim to being the 'great democracy'; a claim that continues to shape individual dreams and the collective identity, while locating the failure for its realization with individuals and their communities. These two events-Damolola and the American electoral fiasco-raise for us important questions about what it means to live within and alongside those communities character-
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
